industry.name: Semiconductors
industry.description: The full career landscape of semiconductor design, fabrication, and packaging — from wafer fab operators to chip architects, driven by the CHIPS Act and growing US domestic production investment.

roles.title: Process Integration Technician
skills: data logging in manufacturing execution system (MES)
roles.description: Supports process integration engineers by running wafers through experimental process sequences, collecting data, and maintaining cleanroom discipline. Entry point into the process R&D track. CHIPS Act investment is dramatically expanding the number of these roles at Intel, TSMC USA, Samsung Austin, and GlobalFoundries.

roles.title: R&D Process Engineer
roles.description: Develops and optimizes individual process modules (e.g., gate oxide growth, metal deposition) within the chip fabrication flow. Works in a cleanroom environment running split-lot experiments, analyzing electrical results, and iterating toward manufacturing-worthy process recipes. Typical starting role for BS/MS engineers at logic and memory fabs.

roles.title: Device Physics Engineer
roles.description: Deep specialist at the intersection of physics and engineering — models and characterizes transistor and memory device behavior to guide process development decisions. Typically PhD or strong MS with a specialization in semiconductor physics. Critical for companies developing sub-5nm node technologies or novel device architectures (FinFET, GAA, 3D DRAM).

roles.title: Senior Process Integration Engineer
roles.description: Owns the integration of multiple process modules into a coherent, manufacturable process flow — the most technically demanding role in process development. Coordinates across etch, deposition, lithography, and metrology teams to resolve interactions and optimize the full flow. High-demand role as US fabs scale toward high-volume manufacturing.

roles.title: Principal Research Scientist
roles.description: Sets the long-range technical direction for process technology, typically with a PhD and 15+ years of fab experience. Represents the company in external technical forums, drives the research agenda, and is the go-to technical authority for next-generation node development. A rare, high-compensation individual contributor role.

roles.title: Process R&D Manager
roles.description: Manages a team of process engineers and scientists, balancing near-term development milestones with longer-range research investments. Owns the department budget, hiring, and performance management. Typically a PhD-level engineer who has transitioned from deep technical to leadership focus.

roles.title: Yield Improvement Engineer
roles.description: Investigates yield loss systematically — analyzing inline metrology data, defect inspections, and wafer sort results to identify and eliminate yield detractors. A high-impact role: improving yield by even 1% on a high-volume production line can be worth tens of millions annually. Strong analytics and data mining skills are essential.

roles.title: Thin Film Engineer
roles.description: Develops and qualifies thin film deposition processes for metal and dielectric layers in advanced semiconductor devices. Works on PVD, CVD, and ALD tools, optimizing film properties (resistivity, stress, conformality) for integration into the full process flow. A specialized module-level role critical for logic and memory device performance.

roles.title: Etch/Deposition Process Scientist
roles.description: Deep specialist in plasma etch or deposition chemistry, developing the sub-nanometer process control required for leading-edge nodes. At advanced fabs (3nm and below), the plasma-material interactions are complex enough to require PhD-level understanding. A critical role as EUV lithography pushes pattern complexity to new limits.

roles.title: Layout Design Technician
roles.description: Performs physical layout tasks under the supervision of design and physical design engineers — placing standard cells, routing local interconnects, and running design rule checks. An entry-level position into the chip design track, often found at fabless companies and design services firms.

roles.title: RTL Design Engineer
roles.description: Designs digital logic at the register-transfer level — the hardware description that defines how data moves through a chip. Works on processor cores, accelerators, or IP blocks, writing RTL that synthesis tools transform into gate-level netlists. One of the foundational roles in fabless semiconductor companies and chip design groups at IDMs.

roles.title: Verification Engineer
roles.description: Builds the test infrastructure that proves chip designs work correctly before tape-out — the point of no return when the design is sent for manufacturing. Uses Universal Verification Methodology (UVM) to create constrained-random testbenches and coverage models. A re-spin costs $5–50M+ at advanced nodes, making verification one of the most critical and well-compensated engineering functions.

roles.title: Physical Design Engineer
skills: IR drop and power grid analysis
roles.description: Owns the physical implementation of chip designs — placing logic, routing metal layers, and meeting stringent timing and power targets within the area constraints of the die. At advanced nodes (5nm and below), this requires deep understanding of process design rules, EUV lithography constraints, and thermal management. Extremely high demand and well-compensated.

roles.title: VLSI Design Engineer
roles.description: Designs analog, mixed-signal, or custom digital circuits at the transistor level — the highest-precision design work in semiconductor engineering. Develops PLLs, ADCs, DACs, SerDes, and power management circuits that require matching performance across process, voltage, and temperature corners. Top of the compensation scale for individual contributors in the industry.

roles.title: DFT Engineer
roles.description: Designs testability features into chips — scan chains, built-in self-test circuits, and boundary scan — that enable manufacturing test equipment to detect fabrication defects efficiently. DFT adds 5–20% overhead to die area but is non-negotiable for high-volume manufacturing. High-demand role as test cost management becomes critical at advanced nodes.

roles.title: Principal Design Engineer
roles.description: The highest individual contributor design role — owns the architecture and technical quality of major chip programs or the entire design practice. Often the person who makes fundamental micro-architecture decisions that determine a chip's competitiveness for its product lifetime. Total compensation frequently exceeds $300K at top-tier semiconductor companies.

roles.title: Design Engineering Manager
roles.description: Leads a team of design and verification engineers through chip programs from architecture to tape-out. Responsible for schedule, quality, and team health. Typically a technical expert who has transitioned into people management, retaining enough technical depth to make sound architectural trade-off decisions.

roles.title: EDA Tools Engineer
roles.description: Builds and maintains the electronic design automation infrastructure that design engineers depend on — scripting CAD flows, integrating PDKs from foundries, managing tool licenses, and automating repetitive tasks. A force-multiplier role: good EDA infrastructure can significantly shorten design cycle time. High demand as companies migrate to new nodes and new EDA tool suites.

roles.title: Fab Operator
roles.description: Runs wafer lots through fabrication process steps — loading wafers into tools, tracking lots through the MES, and performing visual inspection. The entry role into semiconductor manufacturing and one of the most-posted positions in the industry as new US fabs ramp production. CHIPS Act is directly funding tens of thousands of these positions.

roles.title: Wafer Fab Technician
roles.description: Executes process steps with more technical accountability than a basic fab operator — reading inline metrology results, responding to process alarms, and documenting deviations. A 2-year technical degree accelerates growth into process engineering. One of the primary hiring profiles for new US fabs (TSMC Arizona, Samsung Austin, Micron Boise expansion).

roles.title: Photolithography Technician
roles.description: Operates photolithography equipment (steppers, scanners, track systems) — the most critical and highest-precision process in the fab. Controls exposure parameters, monitors overlay and critical dimension (CD) measurements, and escalates deviations. Litho technicians are among the most in-demand skilled roles as EUV scanners require specialized knowledge.

roles.title: CMP Technician
roles.description: Operates chemical mechanical planarization tools — the process that polishes wafer surfaces flat to enable multi-layer metal interconnect stacking. CMP quality directly impacts device performance and yield. A specialized mid-level technician role that commands premium pay at advanced fabs.

roles.title: Fab Process Engineer
roles.description: Owns a specific process module in high-volume manufacturing — maintaining process health, investigating excursions, implementing improvements, and qualifying new configurations. The engineering backbone of a production fab. High demand and competitive compensation at all major US fab sites including Intel, Micron, GlobalFoundries, and the new TSMC and Samsung facilities.

roles.title: Senior Fab Process Engineer
roles.description: Leads process improvement and qualification activities across complex, high-volume manufacturing flows. Develops and implements advanced process control strategies that reduce variation and improve yield. Mentors junior engineers and serves as the technical expert for customer qualification audits and DCMA/government inspections.

roles.title: Fab Shift Supervisor
roles.description: Manages all fab operations during an assigned shift — ensuring wafer lots flow on schedule, operators follow procedures, and any production disruptions are quickly escalated and resolved. A critical leadership role for experienced technicians ready to move into management without necessarily pursuing an engineering path.

roles.title: Fab Operations Manager
roles.description: Accountable for the full production performance of a fab — cycle time, yield, capacity, safety, and cost. Works with process engineers, equipment engineers, and materials teams to ramp new nodes and maintain mature production. A critical leadership role as new US fabs under CHIPS Act investment enter production ramp phases.

roles.title: Implant Process Engineer
roles.description: Specializes in ion implantation — the process of doping silicon wafers with precisely controlled concentrations of impurities to create transistor junctions. Combines deep knowledge of implant tool physics with the process integration context of how doping profiles affect transistor performance. A specialized senior role found at IDMs and advanced logic fabs.

roles.title: Equipment Maintenance Technician
roles.description: Performs scheduled preventive maintenance and basic corrective maintenance on semiconductor process equipment — the technical backbone that keeps fab tools running. Works under the supervision of equipment engineers and learns the tool physics through hands-on maintenance. One of the key entry-level hiring profiles for new US fabs.

roles.title: Equipment Engineer
roles.description: Owns the operational performance of specific equipment — managing preventive maintenance schedules, troubleshooting failures, and working with tool OEMs on hardware upgrades and issues. Equipment downtime translates directly into lost wafer starts and revenue, making this one of the highest-urgency roles in the fab. Strong demand at all new US fab build-outs.

roles.title: Senior Equipment Engineer
roles.description: Leads equipment qualification programs for new tool installs, drives reliability improvement initiatives, and serves as the technical escalation point for complex equipment failures. Owns the highest-impact tools in the fab and mentors junior equipment engineers. Critical hire as US fabs install multibillion-dollar equipment fleets.

roles.title: Equipment Engineering Manager
roles.description: Manages the equipment engineering organization — from maintenance technicians to senior engineers — and owns the capital equipment budget for new installs, upgrades, and spares. Plays a central role in fab ramp planning, coordinating tool deliveries, installation, qualification, and technology transfer from development to manufacturing.

roles.title: Metrology Equipment Engineer
roles.description: Specializes in the equipment that measures process results rather than processing wafers directly — CD SEMs, overlay tools, film thickness gauges, and defect inspection systems. Ensures metrology equipment is calibrated, qualified, and feeding accurate data into the fab's process control and APC systems. Precision and analytical rigor are paramount.

roles.title: Facilities Engineer
roles.description: Maintains and operates the critical support infrastructure that fab process tools depend on — cleanroom HVAC, ultrapure water systems, specialty gas distribution, and chemical waste treatment. A less visible but absolutely essential role: a cleanroom HVAC failure can shut down a billion-dollar fab line within hours.

roles.title: Test Technician
roles.description: Operates automated test equipment (ATE) in wafer sort or final test — loading wafers or packages, running test programs, and logging results. Entry point into the test engineering track, with clear growth paths into ATE test engineering and failure analysis. Growing demand as chip volumes increase and test complexity at advanced nodes requires more hands.

roles.title: ATE Test Engineer
skills: test program development (Teradyne IG-XL, Advantest T2000)
correlating ATE data to device specs
roles.description: Develops and maintains the test programs that run on automated test equipment to classify devices as pass or fail. Balances test coverage (finding real defects) with test time (cost per device). Works across wafer sort and final test and collaborates with design engineers to ensure test programs catch the failure modes that matter most.

roles.title: Package Design Engineer
skills: IC package design (QFN, BGA, CSP, flip-chip)
roles.description: Designs the packaging that protects and connects semiconductor dies to PCBs — from simple QFN and BGA packages to advanced 2.5D/3D architectures using silicon interposers and HBM memory stacking. Packaging is increasingly a competitive differentiator, especially for AI accelerators and high-bandwidth compute applications. High demand and compensation reflect the technical complexity.

roles.title: Failure Analysis Engineer
roles.description: Investigates device failures at the physical level — using advanced microscopy, deprocessing techniques, and electrical probing to identify the exact mechanism causing a chip to fail. Findings directly guide process and design improvements. A detective role requiring deep knowledge of both the fab process and device physics. Critical for quality and customer returns.

roles.title: Quality Engineer (Semi)
roles.description: Manages quality systems and customer quality programs — ensuring products meet specification and that quality data is communicated clearly to demanding customers in automotive, defense, and communications markets. AEC-Q100 automotive qualification experience is particularly valuable as semiconductor content in vehicles continues to grow.

roles.title: Quality Systems Manager (Semi)
roles.description: Owns the quality management system and leads a team of quality engineers across product and process quality functions. Primary contact for Tier 1 automotive and defense customer quality audits. Drives zero-defect culture and manages the quality systems that are table-stakes for selling into automotive, aerospace, and defense end markets.

roles.title: Wafer Sort Engineer
roles.description: Owns the wafer sort test flow — the critical step between wafer fab and packaging where each die is electrically tested and classified. Manages probe card performance, optimizes test programs for yield and cost, and correlates sort results with fab process data to identify yield loss mechanisms. A high-leverage role: catching defective die at sort prevents costly packaging of bad devices.

roles.title: Reliability Engineer
roles.description: Qualifies devices for lifetime reliability — designing and executing accelerated aging tests (HTOL, HAST, thermal cycling) that simulate years of operation in weeks. Demonstrates that devices meet customer lifetime requirements and identifies latent reliability risks before product launch. Increasingly important for automotive and aerospace markets with 15+ year reliability requirements.

roles.title: Test Development Engineer
roles.description: Develops the test specifications and methodologies used in ATE programs — working from device datasheets and design team inputs to define what 'pass' means for every electrical parameter. Bridges design and manufacturing test, ensuring ATE programs reflect device design intent while remaining manufacturable in terms of test time and cost.

roles.title: Packaging Process Engineer
roles.description: Develops and qualifies the manufacturing processes that assemble semiconductor dies into their final packages — wire bonding, flip-chip bonding, underfill, molding, and singulation. Advanced packaging (chiplet integration, 2.5D/3D stacking) is a rapidly growing specialization as companies like Intel (Foveros), AMD (3D V-Cache), and Samsung invest heavily in heterogeneous integration.

roles.title: Product Engineering Manager
roles.description: Manages the product engineering function covering test, packaging, and quality — owning the yield, cost, and quality performance of shipped products. Interfaces with customers on qualification and quality issues, with the design team on new product introduction, and with operations on yield improvement. A senior leadership role bridging engineering and business outcomes.

roles.title: Metrology Specialist (Semi)
skills: inline metrology tool operation (CD SEM, ellipsometer, XRF)
roles.description: Operates the inline metrology and inspection tools that monitor wafer quality throughout the fab process — CD SEMs, film thickness gauges, overlay tools, and defect inspection systems. Generates the measurement data that process and equipment engineers use to control the process. A technically rigorous role that sits at the intersection of test and process operations.

pathways.name: Fab Operator to Process Engineer
pathways.description: Starting on the production floor and growing into process engineering — the most common career progression in semiconductor manufacturing.

pathways.name: Equipment Technician to Engineer
pathways.description: Building from hands-on maintenance expertise into full equipment engineering ownership, with paths into senior roles and management.

pathways.name: Chip Design Track
pathways.description: From layout technician through RTL and physical design to principal design engineering — the path for semiconductor design engineers.

pathways.name: Verification & EDA Track
pathways.description: Specializing in design verification and EDA infrastructure — proving chip correctness and building the tools that make design teams more productive.

pathways.name: Test & Quality Track
pathways.description: From test technician through ATE engineering and quality management — ensuring that every chip shipped meets specification.

pathways.name: Process R&D Track
pathways.description: Building deep expertise in semiconductor process development — from technician support through module engineering to leading the process research roadmap.

pathways.name: Device & Yield Engineering Track
pathways.description: Focusing on device physics and yield — understanding why devices work (or don't), and systematically improving the fraction of wafers that produce good die.

pathways.name: Advanced Packaging Track
pathways.description: Specializing in semiconductor packaging — from test development through package design, packaging process engineering, and product management.

pathways.name: Failure Analysis & Reliability Track
pathways.description: Building expertise in why devices fail and how to prevent it — from test operations through failure analysis and reliability engineering.

pathways.name: Fab Operations Leadership Track
pathways.description: Growing from production floor experience into fab operations management — a path for technically strong individuals who want to lead large manufacturing organizations.
